description: Explains how pytest turns fixture parameters and parametrize marks into collected test cases, how per-parameter marks are stored on items, and how skip, xfail, and marker selection consume those marks.
document:
Pytest treats fixtures, parametrization, and marks as connected collection-time features rather than separate mechanisms. The user-facing docs describe fixtures as named dependencies requested through test function arguments, and describe parametrization as a way to run the same test with several value sets. In the implementation, this connection is strongest during Python test collection: the collector creates a `Metafunc` object for each function, calls the normal hook plus module-level and class-level implementations, and lets those hooks add planned invocations before real test items are yielded.

Fixture parameters enter the same path as explicit test parametrization. A fixture declared with parameters stores those values and optional ids on its fixture marker in `fixtures.py`. During fixture-driven generation, pytest scans the fixture names needed by the test and, when the closest relevant fixture definition has parameter values, calls the metafunc parametrization method indirectly with the fixture name, the fixture's values, its scope, and its ids. This is why parametrized fixtures produce separate test cases and why the selected value later appears to the fixture as request data rather than as a direct function argument value.

Explicit parametrization is normalized through `ParameterSet`, the small record that carries one value set, any marks attached to that set, and an optional id. The public `pytest.param` helper builds these records and is also the documented way to mark a single case inside either a parametrized test function or a parametrized fixture. The helper rejects using the fixture-activation mark on a single parameter set, because that mark would not make sense as a per-case value annotation. Empty parameter lists are not dropped; pytest creates one synthetic parameter set carrying the configured empty-parameter behavior, commonly skip, so collection still produces a meaningful item and report.

The metafunc parametrization method validates argument names and value counts, resolves ids, decides whether each argument is direct or indirect, and then expands the current planned calls into a Cartesian product when multiple parametrization calls are stacked. Each resulting `CallSpec2` records the argument values, parameter indexes, effective parameter scopes, generated id parts, and the marks that came from the selected parameter set. For direct parameters, pytest registers artificial fixture definitions so later setup code can still use the fixture machinery uniformly. The scope module provides the ordered scope model used here for grouping and caching decisions.

Once collection finishes, each planned call becomes a `Function` item. The collector appends the generated id to the item name when there is an id list, binds the call spec to the item, and adds the call spec's marks to the item's own markers. That last step is the bridge from parametrization to ordinary mark behavior: a skip, xfail, or custom mark placed on one parameter value becomes visible through the same marker iteration APIs as a decorator mark on the function, class, or module. Tests in `testing/test_mark.py` cover this by checking that custom marks from parametrized values are visible from the collected item.

Marks themselves are represented by immutable-looking mark objects and lightweight decorators produced by `pytest.mark`. A mark decorator either stores a mark on a test function or class when called as a normal decorator, or returns a combined decorator when called with arguments. The storage helper refuses to put marks on fixture functions, and the fixture decorator likewise refuses functions that already carry marks, matching the deprecation behavior tested around applying parametrization or fixture-use marks to fixtures. The mark generator consults configured marker names, warns about unknown custom marks, and can turn those warnings into collection errors when strict marker validation is enabled.

Runtime skip and xfail handling consumes the same item markers. The skipping plugin evaluates conditional skip marks before unconditional skip marks, requiring a reason for boolean conditions, and raises a skip outcome during setup when one applies. It evaluates xfail marks into a cached result with reason, run behavior, strictness, and expected exception information, then adjusts setup, call, and report outcomes accordingly. As a result, a value marked through `pytest.param` in a fixture parameter list can skip only that generated invocation, while the other generated invocations run normally and report under their own parameter ids.
